items.q: When addressing a stranger or someone older formally, which 'you' do you use?
items.correct: Sie (always capitalised, formal singular and plural).
items.why: Students default to du. German has THREE 'you' forms: du (informal singular), ihr (informal plural), Sie (formal singular OR plural - capitalised). Using du with a stranger or older person is rude. Sie takes third-person plural verb forms.

A devastating faux ami. Bekommen looks like 'become' but means RECEIVE. 'Ich bekomme ein Geschenk' = I receive a present. To become = WERDEN. Confusion: 'I became a present' instead of 'I received a present'.

items.q: How do you say 'my sister' in German?
items.why: Students forget possessive endings. Possessives (mein, dein, sein, ihr, unser, euer) take the SAME ENDINGS as ein/eine. Schwester is feminine = meine. Mein Bruder (masc), meine Schwester (fem), mein Kind (neut), meine Eltern (pl).
The answer examiners want is: Hard-working. “Lazy” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: Ich habe blaue Augen. “Ich bin blaue Augen” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many personality adjectives are listed in the concepts (lustig, schüchtern, fleißig)?
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
